text: The surgeon identifies the pelvic space anatomy and ureter, using a vessel sealer to dissect the uterine attachments. The bladder is mobilized, and the osteoprotonium is dissected. The uterine artery is secured, and the cervix is separated from the vagina to form the vault through calbotomy. The uterus, along with adenexial tissue, is removed. The vault and port sites are sutured.

The surgeon manipulates the bowel out of the pelvis and transsects the ligament, proceeding to open the anterior leaflet of the broad ligament and the anterior peritoneal reflection of the uterus. The bladder is reflected anteriorly using atraumatic forceps. The infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament is transected, with careful attention to secure the ureter. The uterus is then elevated and directed posteriorly.

The surgeon reflects the bladder anteriorly, preparing for a vaginal vault closure. The colpotomy is performed two centimeters below the colpotomizer using a monopolar hook or spatula, opening the vaginal fornices.

The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina while dissecting the bladder by pushing the vesical fascia down using scissors and a uterine manipulator. The uterine artery is approached for transection using a bipolar device.

The surgeon transects the IP ligament on one side, followed by the round ligament and IP ligament on the other side, ensuring complete hemostasis. The uterine vessel is approached in the gray zone using the wand technique for skeletonization, with all actions performed under direct vision.

The surgeon lifts the ovary and approaches the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament, ensuring all steps are performed under direct vision. The ligature is used for transection, dissection, and coagulation, maintaining a bloodless field with bipolar energy.

The uterine manipulator is positioned to protrude over the edge of the table, facilitating movement. The surgeon performs skeletonization of the uterine artery using a bipolar device, ensuring the working angle is maintained at seventy degrees towards the pelvis. The patient's arm is secured, and a catheter is connected to a urinary bag.
The surgeon begins making precise cuts using a monopolar hook to perform the colpotomy and incise the vaginal fornices. The monopolar hook is set to a pure cutting waveform at sixty watts.
The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina to maintain hemoprotein during suturing. The cut is made with precision, highlighting the colpotomy cup pushed up firmly on top of the vaginal fornices.
The uterine manipulator is visible, aiding in the complete excision and creation of the vaginal fold. The extraction process begins, and if the uterus is large, an endo knife is not required.

The surgeon prepares for electrical or mechanical morcellation before closing the vaginal vault, using laparoscopic suturing techniques with a delayed absorbable suture and a needle holder with a specific arm length.

The surgeon designs the vaginal fold, emphasizing its importance in preventing vault prolapse. The laparoscopic closure of the vaginal cuff is performed at the end of the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, aiming to reduce vaginal wall defects.
The surgeon lifts the vaginal vault using sutures placed in the mid cuff, performing hemostasis repeatedly to control bleeding. Copious irrigation is applied to the surgical area.
